content.aim: The '2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure' replaces the '2013 ACCF/AHA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure' and the '2017 ACC/AHA/HFSA Focused Update of the 2013 ACCF/AHA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure.' The guideline is intended to provide patient-centric recommendations for clinicians to prevent, diagnose, and manage patients with heart failure.
content.methods: A comprehensive literature search was conducted from May 2020 to December 2020, encompassing studies, reviews, and other evidence published in English from MEDLINE (PubMed), EMBASE, the Cochrane Collaboration, the Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality, and other relevant databases. Additional relevant clinical trials and research studies, published through September 2021, were also considered.
content.structure: Heart failure is a leading cause of morbidity and mortality globally. The 2022 heart failure guideline provides recommendations based on contemporary evidence for treatment. It presents an evidence-based approach to managing patients with heart failure, intending to improve quality of care and align with patients' interests. Many recommendations from earlier guidelines have been updated with new evidence, with new recommendations created when supported by published data. Value statements are provided for certain treatments supported by high-quality published economic analyses.

content.Summary: The guideline provides recommendations for managing heart failure (HF) with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and highlights medication classes such as sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors (SGLT2i). It addresses the management of comorbidities, special populations, and performance measurement. Key areas include updated treatment recommendations for amyloid heart disease, the importance of monitoring filling pressures, and guidance for patients with HF and various comorbid conditions.
Top Messages: Guideline-directed medical therapy for HFrEF includes SGLT2i.
SGLT2i are recommended for mildly reduced ejection fraction HF (HFmrEF).
New recommendations for HFpEF focus on SGLT2i, MRAs, and ARNis.
Improved LVEF terminology for patients whose LVEF >40%.
Value statements created for selected recommendations.
New treatment recommendations for amyloid heart disease.
Emphasis on noninvasive and invasive testing for increased filling pressures.
Referral to HF specialty team for patients with advanced HF.
Use of revised terminologies for HF staged at risk or pre-HF.
Recommendations for HF patients with various comorbidities.

content.Preamble: Since 1980, the ACC and AHA have translated evidence into clinical practice guidelines to improve cardiovascular health. The focus is on integrating clinical evidence with recommendations, which guides patient care without commercial support.
content.Intended Use: Guidelines are designed for patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD) with the focus on worldwide medical practice.
content.Clinical Implementation: Management should follow guidelines with individual engagement in selection of interventions.
content.Methodology and Modernization: Guidelines are continuously updated with ongoing reviews and adaptations to new evidence and technology.
content.Selection of Committee Members: A diverse committee is selected to ensure comprehensive guideline development.
content.Relationships with Industry and Other En: Policies ensure documents are developed without bias or improper influence.
content.Evidence Review and Committees: Guidelines are developed using randomized trials, cohort studies, and expert opinion. Independent review committees assess critical questions.

content.Guideline Purpose: The purpose of the 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Heart Failure Guideline is to update and consolidate previous guidelines for the management of heart failure. It incorporates recommendations relevant to heart failure and consolidates evidence from previous related guidelines.
content.Methodology and Evidence Review: The guideline recommendations are evidence-based, with the literature review covering research published in English and indexed in major databases from May 2020 to December 2020.

Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction

Pharmacological treatments including ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, etc.
content.Document Review and Approval: Reviewed by officials from AHA, ACC, and HFSA, including content reviewers.
content.Scope: The guideline harmonizes with other ACC/AHA guidelines and covers prevention and management of heart failure, with recommendations based on extensive evidence review.

focus_areas: Management strategies in stage C HF, including: new treatment strategies in HF with sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors (SGLT2i) and angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitors (ARNi), management of HF and atrial fibrillation (AF), including ablation of AF, management of HF and secondary mitral regurgitation (MR), including mitral valve transcatheter edge-to-edge repair
Specific management strategies, including cardiac amyloidosis and cardio-oncology

Class of Recommendation and Level of Evi.Class of Recommendation: Indicates the strength of recommendation, encompassing the estimated magnitude and certainty of benefit in proportion to risk.
Class of Recommendation and Level of Evi.Level of Evidence: Rates the quality of scientific evidence supporting the intervention based on the type, quantity, and consistency of data from clinical trials and other sources.

Class 1 (Strong).Comparative Effectiveness: Treatment/strategy A is recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B

Class 2a (Moderate).Comparative Effectiveness: Treatment/strategy A is probably recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B

Suggested Phrases: Usefulness/effectiveness is unknown/unclear/uncertain or not well-established

Level of Evidence.Level A: High-quality evidence from more than 1 RCT
Level of Evidence.Level B-R (Randomized): Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more RCTs
Level of Evidence.Level B-NR (Nonrandomized): Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more well-designed observational studies
Level of Evidence.Level C-LD (Limited Data): Randomized or nonrandomized observational studies with design/execution limitations
Level of Evidence.Level C-EO (Expert Opinion): Consensus of expert opinion based on clinical experience

Definition of HF.HF Description: HF is a complex clinical syndrome with symptoms and signs resulting from structural or functional impairment of ventricular filling or ejection of blood. Asymptomatic patients with structural heart disease or cardiomyopathies are also considered at risk for HF.
Definition of HF.Stages of HF: ACC/AHA stages of HF emphasize the development and progression of disease. Stages include modifying risk factors (stage A), treating risk and structural heart disease (stage B), and reducing symptoms, morbidity, and mortality (stages C and D).

genes.context: PPAR-γ is mentioned as part of the abbreviation list for heart failure treatment involving receptor targets.

content.Stage A: At Risk for HF: Individuals are at risk for heart failure (HF) but do not show symptoms, structural heart disease, or cardiac biomarkers of stretch or injury. This includes patients with hypertension, atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (CVD), diabetes, metabolic syndrome, obesity, exposure to cardiotoxic agents, genetic variants for cardiomyopathy, or a family history of cardiomyopathy.
content.Stage B: Pre-HF: No symptoms or signs of heart failure, but with evidence of risk such as structural heart disease (e.g., reduced left or right ventricular systolic function, reduced ejection fraction, ventricular hypertrophy, chamber enlargement), valvular diseases, or evidence for increased filling pressures.
content.Stage C: Symptomatic HF: Structural heart disease with current or previous symptoms of heart failure.
content.Stage D: Advanced HF: Marked symptoms of heart failure that interfere with daily life, along with recurrent hospitalizations despite guideline-directed medical therapy.
content.NYHA Classification: Used to categorize symptoms and functional capacity of patients with symptomatic or advanced heart failure. It predicts mortality and determines eligibility for treatment strategies.

genes.context: Patients at risk for heart failure include those with genetic variants for cardiomyopathy, such as ACTA2.

ACC/AHA Stages of Heart Failure.Stage A: At-Risk for Heart Failure - Patients at risk for HF but without current or previous symptoms/signs of HF and without structural/functional heart disease or abnormal biomarkers. Includes patients with hypertension, CVD, diabetes, obesity, exposure to cardiotoxic agents, genetic variant for cardiomyopathy, or family history of cardiomyopathy.
ACC/AHA Stages of Heart Failure.Stage B: Pre-Heart Failure - Patients without current or previous symptoms/signs of HF but evidence of structural heart disease, evidence of increased filling pressures, risk factors, increased natriuretic peptide levels or persistently elevated cardiac troponin in the absence of competing diagnoses.
ACC/AHA Stages of Heart Failure.Stage C: Symptomatic Heart Failure - Patients with current or previous symptoms/signs of HF.
ACC/AHA Stages of Heart Failure.Stage D: Advanced Heart Failure - Marked HF symptoms that interfere with daily life and with recurrent hospitalizations despite attempts to optimize GDMT.

Trajectory of Stage C HF.New Onset/De Novo HF: Newly diagnosed HF, no previous history of HF.
Trajectory of Stage C HF.Resolution of Symptoms: Resolution of symptoms/signs of HF. HF in remission with resolution of previous structural and/or functional heart disease.
Trajectory of Stage C HF.Persistent HF: Persistent HF with ongoing symptoms/signs and/or limited functional capacity.

genes.context: Patients with hypertension, CVD, diabetes, obesity, exposure to cardiotoxic agents, genetic variant for cardiomyopathy, or family history of cardiomyopathy.

content.topic: Classification of Heart Failure by Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF)
content.classification: Patients with heart failure (HF) can be classified based on LVEF due to varying prognoses and responses to treatments. HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) is defined as LVEF ≤40%. HF with preserved EF (HFpEF) has various thresholds but often includes LVEF ≥50%. HF with mid-range EF (HFmrEF) includes EF between 41% and 49%. The trajectory of LVEF over time is crucial in understanding prognosis.

HFrEF.Description: Heart Failure with reduced Ejection Fraction

HFimpEF.Description: Heart Failure with improved Ejection Fraction
HFimpEF.Criteria: Previous LVEF ≤40% and a follow-up measurement of LVEF >40%

HFmrEF.Description: Heart Failure with mildly reduced Ejection Fraction

HFpEF.Description: Heart Failure with preserved Ejection Fraction

content.Additional Context: Evidence of spontaneous or provokable increased LV filling pressures (e.g., elevated natriuretic peptide, noninvasive and invasive hemodynamic measurement) is considered for classification.

content.Section: 2.3 Diagnostic Algorithm for Classification of HF According to LVEF
content.Overview: This section discusses the structural and functional alterations of the heart as the underlying cause for the classification of HFmrEF and HFpEF.
content.Criteria for Diagnosis: The criteria include elevated levels of natriuretic peptides, echocardiographic diastolic parameters, and invasive hemodynamic measurements.
content.Structural Alterations: Increase in left atrial size and volume, and/or an increase in LV mass.
content.Exercise Stress Testing: Used with echocardiographic evaluation of diastolic parameters if the diagnosis is uncertain.
content.H2FPEF Score: A clinical composite score to diagnose HFpEF, integrating variables like obesity, atrial fibrillation, age >60, treatment with antihypertensive medications, echocardiographic measures, and scoring for likelihood.
content.European Society of Cardiology: Developed a diagnostic algorithm involving pretest assessment of HF symptoms and signs, typical clinical demographics, laboratory tests, ECG, and echocardiography.

Trends in Mortality and Hospitalization: Age-adjusted death rate per capita for HF increased.
U.S. hospitalizations for HF increased from 2013 to 2017.
Racial and Ethnic Disparities: Higher death rate per capita for non-Hispanic Black patients compared to non-Hispanic White patients.

general_information: Higher HF incidence among non-Hispanic Black Medicare beneficiaries compared to non-Hispanic White beneficiaries.
Non-Hispanic Black patients with HF have similar or lower HF hospitalization and mortality rates compared to non-Hispanic White patients.
A significant proportion of the U.S. population is at-risk for HF due to factors like hypertension, obesity, diabetes, and atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.
Common causes of HF include ischemic heart disease, myocardial infarction, hypertension, valvular heart disease, and genetic cardiomyopathies.
recommendations: In patients with HF, vital signs and evidence of clinical congestion should be assessed at each encounter.
In patients with symptomatic HF, clinical factors indicating presence of advanced HF should be sought.
For cardiomyopathy patients, obtain a 3-generation family history to assess for inherited disease.
Perform thorough history and physical examination to uncover HF causes and guide disease-specific management.
History and physical examination should identify cardiac/noncardiac disorders, lifestyle and behavioral factors.

content.Synopsis: The document emphasizes the importance of history and physical examination in assessing patients with heart failure (HF). It highlights the role of these examinations in identifying underlying cardiomyopathy, inherited conditions, and potential causes of advanced heart failure. It also mentions the significance of identifying clinical congestion for treatment management and prognosis.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: 1. Clinical congestion can be assessed using various methods and is an important risk factor in HF patients. The document references the PARADIGM-HF study, linking treatment and quality of life improvements.
2. Patients with advanced HF should be identified early for potential interventions. The document suggests focusing on clinical cues indicating advanced HF for appropriate referrals.
3. Familial cardiomyopathy is important for diagnosis in some cases, highlighting the need for detailed family histories and genetic analysis.
4. Specific therapies are recommended for heart failure due to distinct disorders. Correct diagnosis of underlying conditions is crucial.
5. History and physical examinations play a critical role in identifying causes of clinical deterioration in HF patients.

Recommendations.Recommendation: For patients presenting with HF, the specific cause of HF should be explored using additional laboratory testing for appropriate management.

For patients who are diagnosed with HF, laboratory evaluation should include complete blood count, urinalysis, serum electrolytes, blood urea nitrogen, serum creatinine, glucose, lipid profile, liver function tests, iron studies, and thyroid-stimulating hormone to optimize management.
For all patients presenting with HF, a 12-lead ECG should be performed at the initial encounter to optimize management.

content.Synopsis: Laboratory evaluation with complete blood count, urinalysis, serum electrolytes, blood urea nitrogen, serum creatinine, glucose, fasting lipid profile, liver function tests, iron studies, and thyroid-stimulating hormone level and electrocardiography is part of the standard diagnostic evaluation of a patient with HF.
Potential Nonischemic Causes of HF: Endocrine or metabolic (thyroid, acromegaly, pheochromocytoma, diabetes, obesity)
Familial cardiomyopathy or inherited and genetic heart disease
Heart rhythm-related (e.g., tachycardia-mediated, PVCs, RV pacing)
Infiltrative cardiac disease (e.g., amyloid, sarcoid, hemochromatosis)
Myocarditis (infectious, toxin or medication, immunological, hypersensitivity)
content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Identifying the specific cause of HF is important, because conditions that cause HF may require disease-specific therapies. Depending on the clinical suspicion, additional diagnostic studies are usually required to diagnose specific causes such as ischemic cardiomyopathy, cardiac amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, hemochromatosis, infectious mechanisms (e.g., HIV, COVID-19, Chagas), hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, acromegaly, connective tissue disorders, tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy, Takotsubo, peripartum cardiomyopathy, cardiotoxicity with cancer therapies.

genes.context: Thyroid-stimulating hormone level and electrocardiography is part of the standard diagnostic evaluation of a patient with HF.

content.content: The document provides guidelines for the use of biomarkers in the prevention, diagnosis, and risk stratification of heart failure (HF). It emphasizes the utility of B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) and N-terminal prohormone of BNP (NT-proBNP) in various clinical settings. Key recommendations include the measurement of BNP or NT-proBNP in patients presenting with dyspnea to support diagnosis and risk stratification of HF, and its role in establishing prognosis in hospitalized patients. The document also mentions that BNP and NT-proBNP can be used to help prevent HF onset in at-risk patients and to guide treatment management. The document discusses how obesity affects the levels of these biomarkers, potentially reducing diagnostic sensitivity.

General Information: Higher sensitivity of BNP and NT-proBNP for ruling out HF than ruling in.
Increases in BNP and NT-proBNP levels are reported in various cardiac and noncardiac causes.
Higher levels of BNP and NT-proBNP are associated with greater risk for adverse outcomes in patients with HF.
Not all patients need biomarker measurement if they already have advanced HF.
STOP-HF trial: Importance of BNP testing and education on adherence to medication.
BNP-based screening reduced asymptomatic LV dysfunction and cardiac events.
Predischarge BNP and NT-proBNP levels are strong predictors of risk of death or hospitalization for HF.

Cardiac: Toxic-metabolic myocardial insults, including cancer chemotherapy
Noncardiac: Pulmonary: Obstructive sleep apnea, severe pneumonia

Recommendations.Description: In first-degree relatives of selected patients with genetic or inherited cardiomyopathies, genetic screening and counseling are recommended to detect cardiac disease and prompt consideration of treatments to decrease heart failure progression and sudden death.
In select patients with nonischemic cardiomyopathy, referral for genetic counseling and testing is reasonable to identify conditions that could guide treatment for patients and family members.

content.Synopsis: For patients with suspected genetic or inherited cardiomyopathies, a family history should be performed, ideally diagrammed as a family tree. Genetic variants have been found in 25% to 40% of patients with dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) with a positive family history and in 10% to 30% without a recognized family history. Genetic testing is key for risk stratification and treatment decisions, mainly regarding defibrillator use for sudden death prevention and for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and desmosomal variants.
content.Recommendations-Specific Supportive Text: Variants in titin, lamin A/C, and filamin-C are common and linked to various cardiomyopathies and arrhythmias. Genetic confirmation of Fabry’s cardiomyopathy supports enzyme replacement therapy.

genes.context: The most common pathogenic variants identified are truncations in the large structural protein titin, which have been implicated in DCM and also in peripartum or alcoholic cardiomyopathies.
Pathogenic variants in lamin A/C can be associated with conduction block and atrial arrhythmias as well as ventricular arrhythmias.
Filamin-C mutations have been associated with skeletal myopathies and with isolated cardiomyopathy with ventricular arrhythmias.

Patient or Family Member Phenotypic Find: Right ventricular thinning or fatty replacement on imaging or biopsy
Ask Specifically About Family Members: Any mention of cardiomyopathy, enlarged or weak heart, HF
Document even if attributed to other causes, such as alcohol or peripartum cardiomyopathy

Patient or Family Member Phenotypic Find: Abnormal high or low voltage or conduction, and repolarization, altered RV forces
Frequent NSVT or very frequent PVCs
Ask Specifically About Family Members: Sudden death attributed to 'massive heart attack' without known CAD
Unexplained fatal event such as drowning or single-vehicle crash

Patient or Family Member Phenotypic Find: Other possible manifestations of systemic syndromes
Ask Specifically About Family Members: Any known skeletal muscle disease, including mention of Duchenne and Becker's, Emery-Dreifuss limb-girdle dystrophy
Systemic syndromes: Dysmorphic features, Mental retardation, Congenital deafness, Neurofibromatosis, Renal failure with neuropathy

Recommendations for Evaluation With Card: In patients with suspected or new-onset HF, or those presenting with acute decompensated HF, a chest x-ray should be performed to assess heart size and pulmonary congestion and to detect alternative cardiac, pulmonary, and other diseases.
In patients with suspected or newly diagnosed HF, transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) should be performed during initial evaluation to assess cardiac structure and function.
In patients with HF who have had a significant clinical change, repeat measurement of EF, degree of structural remodeling, and valvular function are useful to inform therapeutic interventions.
In patients for whom echocardiography is inadequate, alternative imaging (e.g., CMR, CT, radionuclide imaging) is recommended for assessment of LVEF.
In patients with HF or cardiomyopathy, CMR can be useful for diagnosis or management.
In patients with HF, an evaluation for possible ischemic heart disease can be useful to identify the cause and guide management.

content.summary: Cardiac imaging is crucial for evaluating individuals with suspected heart failure (HF), guiding evidence-based therapy. A comprehensive transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) is the most informative initial test. This document outlines guidelines for evaluating HF, focusing on left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) as a primary assessment tool. The chest x-ray, despite its limitations, can provide additional diagnostic support. Noninvasive stress imaging and echocardiography are recommended for assessing HF, guiding therapy, and monitoring disease progression. Routine LVEF assessment is not indicated without a change in clinical status or therapy response.
recommendations: Use a chest x-ray as a supplemental diagnostic tool for HF symptoms.
Employ TTE for assessing cardiac structure and function.
Utilize serial echocardiograms to monitor heart function and structural remodeling, especially in patients with adverse responses or treatments affecting cardiac function.

points: Reevaluation of heart failure (HF) is useful for determining candidacy for therapies after myocardial infarction or guideline-directed medical therapy.
If transthoracic echocardiography is inadequate, additional imaging modalities like CMR can assess cardiac structure and function.
CMR (Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance) provides a detailed evaluation of cardiac volumes, mass, and ejection fraction without ionizing radiation.
CMR can help in assessing myocardial cause, identifying acute/chronic myocardial infarcts, and diagnosing conditions like CAD.
CMR techniques, such as T-1 and T-2 mapping, can suggest specific cardiomyopathies and measure interstitial space, aiding in prognosis and diagnostics.
Noninvasive imaging techniques can detect significant coronary artery disease (CAD) and evaluate ischemic heart disease.
CAD is a leading cause of HF; imaging techniques can guide revascularization decisions.
Viability imaging may guide revascularization, though routine assessment pre-revascularization isn't generally supported by evidence.
Noninvasive imaging for routine surveillance is appropriate in case of clinical stability.

content.Target Population: Patients with NYHA class III HF
content.Importance: To improve clinical outcomes and reduce HF hospitalizations through remote monitoring of PA pressure.

Recommendations.Details: In selected adult patients with NYHA class III HF and history of HF hospitalization in the past year or elevated natriuretic peptide levels, wireless monitoring of PA pressure by an implanted hemodynamic monitor reduces the risk of HF hospitalizations.
In patients with NYHA class III HF with a recent HF hospitalization, wireless monitoring of PA pressure provides uncertain value.

Summarized Findings: HF is a chronic condition with potential for remote monitoring to improve outcomes.
Previous trials suggest benefits of implanted PA pressure sensors.
Cost-effectiveness studies have shown mixed results regarding CardioMEMS device.
Study References: CHAMPION trial reported significant improvement in hospitalizations.
GUIDE-HF study does not show significant reduction in mortality or HF events.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with HF, assessment and documentation of NYHA functional classification are recommended to determine eligibility for treatments.
In selected ambulatory patients with HF, cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) is recommended to determine appropriateness of advanced treatments (e.g., LVAD, heart transplant).
In ambulatory patients with HF, performing a CPET or 6-minute walk test is reasonable to assess functional capacity.
In ambulatory patients with unexplained dyspnea, CPET is reasonable to evaluate the cause of dyspnea.

content.synopsis: Functional impairment and exercise intolerance are common in HF. CPET and the 6-minute walk test are standardized, reliable, and reproducible tests to quantify functional capacity. The NYHA functional classification can be used to grade the severity of functional limitation and to define candidates for certain treatments.
detailed_recommendations: NYHA function classification is an ordinal, categorical variable used to document functional limitation in patients with cardiac disease, including HF.
Many CPET variables have been associated with prognosis in patients with HF.
Objective assessment of exercise capacity with CPET can be useful in clinical management of patients with HF.

content.title: Clinical Assessment: HF Risk Scoring
content.purpose: To evaluate and manage the prognostic risk in patients with heart failure (HF).
key_points: Patients with HF should have their risk factors assessed to guide discussions on prognosis, goals of care, and treatment.
Risk scores are valuable for ambulatory or hospitalized patients to estimate mortality risk.
Several predictive models are validated using clinical trial data and population-based cohorts.
Models include the Seattle Heart Failure Model, Heart Failure Survival score, and MAGGIC predictive model.
Risk factors include EF (ejection fraction), symptoms, clinical settings, and other variables such as exercise capacity and natriuretic peptide levels.
Strategies like CPET (cardiopulmonary exercise testing) are useful in assessing exercise capacity and differentiating cardiac from other causes of dyspnea.
recommendations: Use validated multivariable risk scores for ambulatory or hospitalized HF patients to predict mortality risk.
Consider models for incident HF prevention in general population assessments.
Use additional models with exercise and peptide levels to predict short-term survival in acute HF patients.

Stage A (Patients at Risk for HF) Recomm: In patients with hypertension, control blood pressure to prevent symptomatic HF.
In patients with type 2 diabetes and high cardiovascular risk, use SGLT2i to prevent hospitalizations for HF.
Promote healthy lifestyle habits to reduce future risk of HF.
For patients at risk, utilize biomarker-based screening to optimize GDMT.
Use multivariable risk scores to estimate risk of incident HF.

content.Synopsis: The guideline emphasizes the role of healthy lifestyle habits, such as regular physical activity, healthy diet, and not smoking in reducing the risk of heart failure (HF). The AHA/ACC primary prevention guidelines suggest recommendations for diet, physical activity, and weight control due to their association with reduced risk of HF. Blood pressure control is crucial, and a target of <130/80 mm Hg is recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease risk. The role of SGLT2i in reducing HF hospitalizations is noted.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.1: Elevated systolic and diastolic blood pressure are major risk factors for symptomatic HF. The importance of blood pressure control in HF prevention is highlighted, with studies showing reductions in HF events with appropriate management.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.2: RCTs in patients with type 2 diabetes and at risk for or with established CVD show that SGLT2i prevent HF hospitalizations. The mechanisms for improvement seem to be independent of glucose lowering.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.3: Healthy lifestyle habits are emphasized to reduce the lifetime risk of HF and preserve cardiac structure. Diet patterns that include more plant-based foods are particularly noted.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.4: A large-scale study (STOP-HF) showed the importance of screening in patients at risk, highlighting the role of BNP testing and monitoring.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.5: Incident HF risk prediction involves diverse models with variables such as age, sex, race, body mass index, and more. These prediction models improve clinical outcomes.

recommendations: Optimal control of blood pressure for patients with hypertension.
Use of SGLT2 inhibitors for patients with type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease (CVD) or high risk for CVD.
Optimal management of CVD for patients with CVD.
Multidisciplinary evaluation for management for patients with exposure to cardiotoxic agents.
Genetic screening and counseling for first-degree relatives of patients with genetic or inherited cardiomyopathies.
Natriuretic peptide biomarker screening for patients at risk.
Use of validated multivariable risk scores for patients at risk.
ACE inhibitors for patients with Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF) ≤50%.
ARB if ACEi intolerant for patients with a recent myocardial infarction (MI) and LVEF ≤40%.
Beta blockers for patients with LVEF ≤40%.
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) for patients with LVEF ≤30%, >1 year survival, >40 days post MI.
Genetic counseling and testing for patients with nonischemic cardiomyopathy.

content.lifestyle_modifications_continuation: Continue lifestyle modifications and management strategies implemented in Stage A, through Stage B.

genes.context: Genetic screening and counseling for first-degree relatives of patients with genetic or inherited cardiomyopathies; Genetic counseling and testing for patients with nonischemic cardiomyopathy.

content.Stage B Management: Focuses on preventing the syndrome of clinical HF in patients with pre-HF. This includes using ACE inhibitors, statins, ARBs, beta blockers, and avoiding certain drugs that may increase risk or be harmful.
Treatment Recommendations: ACE inhibitors for patients with LVEF ≤40% to prevent symptomatic HF.
Statins for patients with recent or remote MI to prevent symptomatic HF.
ARB for patients intolerant to ACE inhibitors.
Beta blockers for certain patients with MI to reduce mortality.
ICD recommended for patients post-MI with LVEF ≤30% to reduce total mortality.
Avoid thiazolidinediones in patients with LVEF <50% due to increased risk.
Avoid nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers in patients with LVEF <50% due to negative inotropic effects.
content.Synopsis: Stage B HF represents asymptomatic structural and functional cardiac abnormalities increasing the risk for symptomatic HF. Identifying at-risk individuals allows for early intervention with lifestyle and pharmacological therapies. Current guidelines recommend therapies mitigating the risk of progression to symptomatic HF.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: ACEi have been shown to impede maladaptive remodeling after acute MI in patients with reduced LVEF. They reduce mortality, HF hospitalizations, and progression to severe HF.
Statins reduce adverse CAD events and the risk of incident HF in patients with MI, ACS, and high cardiovascular risk.
ARB trials have compared ARB with ACEi after MI. ARBs are alternatives for patients with ACEi-induced angioedema.
Beta blockers improve adverse cardiac remodeling and outcomes in patients with asymptomatic reduced LVEF after MI.
The Framingham studies showed increased risk of death in patients with asymptomatic low LVEF compared to those with normal LVEF.
Beta blockers are used in asymptomatic patients with LV systolic dysfunction to prevent adverse LV remodeling.
Thiazolidinediones are associated with fluid retention and increased rates of HF in patients with type 2 diabetes.

Sections.Header: Nonpharmacological Interventions: Self-Care Support in HF

Recommendations.Description: Patients with HF should receive care from multidisciplinary teams to facilitate the implementation of GDMT, address potential barriers to self-care, reduce the risk of subsequent rehospitalization for HF, and improve survival (1-4).
Patients with HF should receive specific education and support to facilitate HF self-care in a multidisciplinary manner (2, 5-9).
In patients with HF, vaccinating against respiratory illnesses is reasonable to reduce mortality (10-16).

In adults with HF, screening for depression (17, 18), social isolation (19-22), frailty (23, 24), and low health literacy (25, 26) as risk factors for poor self-care is reasonable to improve management.

Comments: Avoid thiazolidinediones in patients with reduced LVEF.
Nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers like diltiazem and verapamil are not recommended for patients with HF or reduced LVEF.

content.Synopsis: Heart Failure (HF) management requires a multidisciplinary approach involving cardiologists, nurses, pharmacists, mental health clinicians, and more. Self-care in HF includes taking medications, restricting sodium, staying active, and getting vaccinations. Class I recommendations for patients in stages A and B, and GDMT for stage C.
Key Points: Multidisciplinary interventions reduce hospitalization and mortality.
Self-care includes taking medications, diet, and exercise.
Vaccinations like influenza and pneumococcal are beneficial.
Social factors affect self-care and HF outcomes.
content.Recommendation: Educate HF patients on self-care, endorse vaccinations, and manage complex cases actively. Depression and social isolation should be addressed to improve outcomes.

Interventions: Home health aide
Home meal deliveries
Adult day care
Geriatric psychiatry referral
Memory care support groups
Psychotherapy
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors
Nurse-led support

Screening Tools: Tobacco, Alcohol, Prescription medication, and other Substance use (TAPS)
Interventions: Referral to social work services and community support partners
Referral for addiction psychiatry consultation
Cardiac rehabilitation
Registered dietitian nutritionist evaluation for malnutrition

Screening Tools: Comprehensive Score for Financial Toxicity-Functional Assessment of Chronic Illness Therapy (COST-FACIT)
Interventions: PharmD referral to review prescription assistance eligibilities

Screening Tools: U.S. Household Food Security Survey Module, 6 items
Interventions: Determine eligibility for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
Connect patients with community partners such as food pantries/food banks
Home meal deliveries
Registered dietitian nutritionist evaluation for potential malnutrition
Referral to local housing services
Connect patients with community housing partners

Barriers.Barrier: Intimate partner violence or elder abuse
Interventions: Referral to social work services and community support partners

Barriers.Barrier: Limited English proficiency or other language barriers
Screening Tools: Routinely inquire in which language the patient is most comfortable conversing
Interventions: Access to interpreter services covering a wide range of languages, ideally in person or, alternatively, via video platform
Printed educational materials in a range of appropriate languages

Interventions: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ) Health Literacy Universal Precautions Toolkit
Written education tools provided at sixth grade reading level or below
Graphic educational documents

Support group referral
Referral to social work services
