Maximize opportunities for telehealth visits and remote monitoring

Recommendation for Dietary Sodium Restri.Recommendation: For patients with stage C HF, avoiding excessive sodium intake is reasonable to reduce congestive symptoms.

content.Synopsis: Restricting dietary sodium is a common non-pharmacological treatment for patients with HF symptomatic with congestion. Concerns exist about the quality of data on clinical benefits of sodium restriction. The AHA recommends reducing sodium intake to <2300 mg/d for cardiovascular health. There is no strong evidence supporting this level for patients with HF. Sodium restriction can cause poor dietary quality, and routine supplementation with vitamins or multivitamins has not been proven beneficial.

Recommendations.Recommendations: Exercise training improves functional status, exercise performance, and QOL for patients with HF.
Cardiac rehabilitation programs improve functional capacity and exercise tolerance.

content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: RCTs show aggressive sodium restriction improved certain outcomes, but had inconclusive results in other areas. Exercise training shows benefits in functional status and QOL.
content.Management of Stage C HF: Exercise and cardiac rehabilitation improves physical capacity and QOL. Evidence from RCTs supports this.

content.exercise_training: Exercise training is associated with improvements in functional capacity, exercise duration, health-related quality of life, and reduction in heart failure hospitalizations in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF).

diuretics_recommendations.recommendation: In patients with heart failure who have fluid retention, diuretics are recommended to relieve congestion, improve symptoms, and prevent worsening heart failure.

For patients with heart failure and congestive symptoms, addition of a thiazide to treatment with a loop diuretic should be reserved for patients who do not respond to moderate- or high-dose loop diuretics to minimize electrolyte abnormalities.

content.diuretics_details: Bumetanide, furosemide, and torsemide are loop diuretics that inhibit reabsorption of sodium or chloride at the loop of Henle. Thiazide diuretics such as chlorthalidone or hydrochlorothiazide may be used in patients with hypertension and mild fluid retention. Metolazone or chlorothiazide may be added in refractory edema unresponsive to loop diuretics alone.

content.Diuretics: Used for treatment of congestion in chronic heart failure (HF). Types include loop diuretics (e.g., Bumetanide, Furosemide, Torsemide) and thiazide diuretics (e.g., Chlorthiazide, Chlorthalidone, Hydrochlorothiazide, Indapamide, Metolazone).
content.Treatment Strategy: Diuretic resistance can be managed by escalating loop diuretic dose, intravenous administration, or using different diuretic classes. Combination of thiazide with loop diuretics is noted.
content.Pharmacological Treatment: Recommendations for Renin-Angiotensin System Inhibition with ACEi or ARB or ARNi for patients with HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF).
Recommendations: In patients with HFrEF and NYHA class II to III symptoms, use of ARNi is recommended to reduce morbidity and mortality.
In patients with prior/current symptoms of chronic HFrEF, use of ACEi is beneficial.
In patients intolerant to ACEi, use of ARB is recommended.
When ARNi is not feasible, an ACEi or ARB is advised for those with chronic HFrEF symptoms.

guidelines.recommendation: In patients with chronic symptomatic HFrEF NYHA class II or III who tolerate an ACEi or ARB, replacement by an ARNI is recommended to further reduce morbidity and mortality.
In patients with chronic symptomatic HFrEF, treatment with an ARNI instead of an ACEi provides high economic value.
ARNI should not be administered concomitantly with ACEi or within 36 hours of the last dose of an ACEi.
ARNI should not be administered to patients with any history of angioedema.
ACEi should not be administered to patients with any history of angioedema.

content.synopsis: Inhibition of the renin-angiotensin system is recommended to reduce morbidity and mortality for patients with HFrEF, and ARNi, ACEi, or ARB are recommended as first-line therapy. If patients tolerate ACEi or ARB, they should switch to an ARNI due to improved outcomes. ARB may be used as an alternative to ACEi in intolerable cases, particularly in those with angioedema history. Sacubitril-valsartan is approved for patients with symptomatic HF. Effects and potential off-target effects need consideration.

content.1: The guideline discusses the use of ARNi versus ACE inhibitors for reducing heart failure events post-MI, comparing sacubitril-valsartan to ACEi ramipril.
content.2: Clinical trials support ACEi in HF patients with varying severity and comorbid CAD. Safety concerns include angioedema and electrolyte imbalance.
content.3: ARBs are recommended for HF patients intolerant to ACEi, with evidence from large RCTs showing mortality and hospitalization reduction.
content.4: Cost-effectiveness studies favor ACEi for chronic HF, with few data on ARBs.
content.5: ARBs and ARNi are alternatives, with sacubitril-valsartan showing mortality benefits in certain trials.
content.6: Economic evaluations indicate ARNi's cost remains within acceptable value ranges, needing a minimum treatment duration for cost-effectiveness.

recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with HFrEF and NYHA class II to IV symptoms, an MRA (spironolactone or eplerenone) is recommended to reduce morbidity and mortality, if eGFR is >30 mL/min/1.73 m2 and serum potassium is <5.0 mEq/L. Careful monitoring of potassium, renal function, and diuretic dosing should be performed at initiation and closely monitored thereafter to minimize risk of hyperkalemia and renal insufficiency.
In patients with HFrEF and NYHA class II to IV symptoms, MRA therapy provides high economic value.
In patients taking MRA whose serum potassium cannot be maintained at <5.5 mEq/L, MRA should be discontinued to avoid life-threatening hyperkalemia.

content.synopsis: MRA (also known as aldosterone antagonists or antimineralocorticoids) show consistent improvements in all-cause mortality, HF hospitalizations, and SCD across a wide range of patients with HFrEF. Patients at risk for renal dysfunction or hyperkalemia require close monitoring. The efficacy of MRAs for HFrEF is generally high. Eplerenone has fewer adverse effects compared to spironolactone due its higher selectivity for the aldosterone receptor.
recommendation_support: Based on clinical trials such as RALES, EPHESUS, and EMPHASIS-HF, MRAs show improvements in survival and reduction in symptoms in patients with HFrEF.
Economic analyses demonstrate that MRAs are cost-effective, with a cost per QALY under $1000.

content.MRA uses: Spironolactone and eplerenone are primarily excreted through the kidneys. Concerns about safety exist when eGFR is ≤30 mL/min/1.73 m². Co-administration of MRA with ACEi or ARB increases hyperkalemia risk.
content.SGLT2i recommendations: For patients with symptomatic chronic HFrEF, SGLT2i are recommended to reduce hospitalization and cardiovascular mortality, irrespective of type 2 diabetes presence.

content.clinical_guideline: This guideline provides recommendations for the use of hydralazine and isosorbide dinitrate in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), particularly for African American patients. The combination is recommended for those with NYHA class III-IV HFrEF receiving optimal medical therapy, as it improves symptoms and reduces morbidity and mortality. For patients intolerant to ACEi or ARB, this combination should be considered.
content.trial_information: Two RCTs, V-HeFT I and A-HeFT, demonstrated benefits of hydralazine and isosorbide dinitrate in self-identified African Americans. The A-HeFT was stopped early due to significant benefits, despite potential issues with extrapolating the results to wider populations due to the termination timing based on remarkable benefit. The economic value of this treatment was also acknowledged.

content.Title: Treatment of HFrEF Stages C and D

Steps Overview.Description: Establish diagnosis of HFrEF. Address congestion, initiate GDMT.
Titrate to target dosing as tolerated, considering lab results, health status, and LVEF.
Implement additional GDMT and device therapy as indicated.
Reassess symptoms, labs, health status, and LVEF.
Referral for HF specialty care for additional therapy.

Medications and Therapies: ARNI in NYHA II-III; ACEI or ARB in NYHA II-IV

Patient Scenarios.Group: NYHA II-III; ambulatory IV; LVEF ≤35%; NSR and QRS ≥150 ms with LBBB

Further Options: Palliative care (can be initiated before Stage D)

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with HF class II to IV symptoms, omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA) supplementation may be reasonable to use as adjunctive therapy to reduce mortality and cardiovascular hospitalizations.

In patients with HF who experience hyperkalemia while taking a renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system inhibitor (RAASi), the effectiveness of potassium binders (patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) to improve outcomes by facilitating continuation of RAASi therapy is uncertain.
In patients with chronic HFrEF without a specific indication, anticoagulation is not recommended.

synopsis: Trials show that omega-3 PUFA supplementation results in a 10% to 20% risk reduction in fatal and nonfatal cardiovascular events.
Hyperkalemia is common in HF and can lead to arrhythmias and underuse of GDMT.
Newer potassium-binding agents can lower potassium levels and enable treatment with a RAASi in patients with HF.
recommendation_specific_text: GISSI-HF showed a reduction in death with omega-3 PUFA in symptomatic patients with HF.
The REDUCE-IT trial showed a reduction in cardiovascular events with 2 g of icosapent ethyl.
other_content: The outcome of death or admission to hospital for a cardiovascular event was significantly reduced with omega-3 PUFA.

content.Title: Drugs of Unproven Value or That May Worsen HF

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with HFrEF, dihydropyridine calcium channel-blocking drugs are not recommended treatment for HF.
In patients with HFrEF, vitamins, nutritional supplements, and hormonal therapy are not recommended other than to correct specific deficiencies.
In patients with HFrEF, nondihydropyridine calcium channel-blocking drugs are not recommended.
In patients with HFrEF, class IC antiarrhythmic medications and dronedarone may increase the risk of mortality.
In patients with HFrEF, thiazolidinediones increase the risk of worsening HF symptoms and hospitalizations.

In patients with type 2 diabetes and high cardiovascular risk, the dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitors saxagliptin and alogliptin increase the risk of HF hospitalization and should be avoided in patients with HF.
In patients with HFrEF, NSAIDs worsen HF symptoms and should be avoided or withdrawn whenever possible.

content.Synopsis: There is strong evidence for benefit with selected medications for HFrEF, but several classes of medications have either unproven value or potential harm. These include calcium channel blockers, antiarrhythmic agents, NSAIDs, medications for diabetes, and nutritional supplements.

content.1: Second-generation dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers, like amlodipine and felodipine, target calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle cells. The PRAISE-1 trial showed mortality reduction in patients with Amlodipine, but PRAISE-2 found no survival benefit. Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers might help hypertension patients despite optimized GDMT.
content.2: Nutritional supplements for HF, such as vitamin D and coenzyme Q10, show limited evidence or no significant effect on mortality. Coenzyme Q10 was studied in the Q-SYMBIO trial with no change in NYHA class but reduced adverse events.
content.3: Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers like diltiazem and verapamil might not be tolerated in HF. Verapamil had no impact on survival post-MI, and diltiazem had no impact on mortality in HFpEF but increased risk post-MI.
content.4: CAST trial showed increased mortality with class IC antiarrhythmics in certain conditions, but amiodarone and dofetilide showed neutral effects in HFpEF. SWORD trial indicated potential harm with sotalol.
content.5: Thiazolidinediones enhance insulin sensitivity and may lead to fluid retention, potentially increasing cardiovascular events in HF or diabetic patients.
content.6: DPP-4 inhibitors have been associated with increased HF hospitalization risk. The trial involving Saxagliptin highlighted concerns, particularly in patients with no prior HF.

content.table_title: Selected Prescription Medications That May Cause or Exacerbate HF

medications.Possible Mechanism(s): Prostaglandin inhibition leading to sodium and water retention, increased systemic vascular resistance, and blunted response to diuretics
Prostaglandin inhibition leading to sodium and water retention, increased systemic vascular resistance, and blunted response to diuretics

Subcategories.Possible Mechanism(s): Beta-1-receptor stimulation with increases in renin and aldosterone

general_recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with HFrEF, titration of guideline-directed medication dosing to achieve target doses shown to be efficacious in RCTs is recommended, to reduce cardiovascular mortality and HF hospitalizations, unless not well tolerated.

In patients with HFfEF, titration and optimization of guideline-directed medications as frequently as every 1 to 2 weeks depending on the patient’s symptoms, vital signs, and laboratory findings can be useful to optimize management.

content.title: Drugs Commonly Used for HFrEF (Stage C HF)

drugs.name: Sacubitril–valsartan
drugs.initial_dose: 49 mg sacubitril and 51 mg valsartan twice daily
drugs.target_dose: 97 mg sacubitril and 103 mg valsartan twice daily

drugs.name: Fixed dose combination
drugs.initial_dose: 20 mg isosorbide dinitrate and 37.5 mg hydralazine 3 times daily
drugs.target_dose: 40 mg isosorbide dinitrate and 75 mg hydralazine 3 times daily

drugs.name: Isosorbide dinitrate and hydralazine
drugs.initial_dose: 20–30 mg isosorbide dinitrate and 25–50 mg hydralazine 3–4 times daily
drugs.target_dose: 120 mg isosorbide dinitrate daily in divided doses and 300 mg hydralazine total daily in divided doses

content.synopsis: Clinical trials of ACEi, ARB, ARNi, beta blockers, and most other HFrEF medications had therapy initiated at low dose by trial protocol. If the initial dose was tolerated, the protocol would then direct the up titration of medication dose over time to a specified target dose unless not well tolerated.

content.title: Benefits of Evidence-Based Therapies for Patients With HFrEF

context.text: Use of all 4 drug classes has been estimated to reduce all-cause mortality by 73% compared with no treatment. Initiation and titration should be individualized and optimized without delay according to patient’s symptoms, vital signs, functional status, tolerance, renal function, electrolytes, comorbidities, specific cause of HF, and ability of follow-up.

7.3.9.1. Management of Stage C HF: Ivabr.RECOMMENDATION: For patients with symptomatic (NYHA class II to III) stable chronic HFrEF (LVEF ≤35%) who are receiving GDMT, including a beta blocker at maximum tolerated dose, and who are in sinus rhythm with a heart rate of ≥70 bpm at rest, ivabradine can be beneficial to reduce HF hospitalizations and cardiovascular death.
7.3.9.1. Management of Stage C HF: Ivabr.Synopsis: Heart rate is a strong predictor of cardiovascular outcomes in the general population and in patients with CVD, including HF. The SHIFT (Ivabradine and Outcomes in Chronic Heart Failure) trial tested the hypothesis that reducing heart rate in patients with HF improves cardiovascular outcomes. SHIFT demonstrated the efficacy of ivabradine, a sinoatrial node modulator that selectively inhibits the I₁ current, in reducing the composite endpoint of cardiovascular death or HF hospitalization in patients with HF.
7.3.9.1. Management of Stage C HF: Ivabr.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: The greatest benefit in SHIFT was a reduction in HF hospitalization.

7.3.9.2. Pharmacological Treatment for S.RECOMMENDATION: In patients with symptomatic HFrEF despite GDMT (or who are unable to tolerate GDMT), digoxin might be considered to decrease hospitalizations for HF.
7.3.9.2. Pharmacological Treatment for S.Synopsis: To date, there has been only 1 large-scale, RCT of digoxin in patients with HF. This trial, which predated current GDMT, primarily enrolled patients with NYHA class II to III HF and showed that treatment with digoxin for 2 to 5 years had no effect on mortality but modestly reduced the combined risk of death and hospitalization.
7.3.9.2. Pharmacological Treatment for S.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Digoxin is usually initiated at a low dose because larger doses are rarely required in the management of HF and are potentially detrimental.

content.Summary: In patients with progression of HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) despite guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT), there may be a role for novel therapeutic agents. An oral soluble guanylate cyclase stimulator, vericiguat, is discussed as a treatment option. The VICTORIA trial studied patients with HFrEF and high risk of worsening, and results showed a potential benefit in reducing heart failure hospitalization and cardiovascular death with vericiguat compared to placebo.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In selected high-risk patients with HFrEF and recent worsening of HF already on GDMT, an oral soluble guanylate cyclase stimulator (vericiguat) may be considered to reduce HF hospitalization and cardiovascular death.

content.Synopsis: Vericiguat binds and stimulates soluble guanylate cyclase (sGC) to increase cGMP production, which has beneficial effects in patients with HF, such as vasodilation and improvement in endothelial function.
content.Study: The VICTORIA trial was a phase III trial that randomized 5050 high-risk patients with worsening HFrEF to receive vericiguat versus placebo. Over a median follow-up of 10.8 months, the primary outcome of cardiovascular death or HF hospitalization was lower in the vericiguat group.
content.Patient Inclusion Criteria: Patients had to have LVEF <45%, NYHA class II to IV, elevated NT-proBNP levels, and recent HF worsening.
content.Key Findings: The reduction in cardiovascular events was significant, although the absolute risk reduction was lower than expected.

Additional Medical Therapies for HFrEF.Ivabradine: Recommended for NYHA II-III, HFrEF, NSR, heart rate ≥70 bpm, on maximally tolerated beta blockers.
Additional Medical Therapies for HFrEF.Vericiguat: Recommended for NYHA II-IV, LVEF <45%, recent HFH, or IV diuretics; elevated NP levels.
Additional Medical Therapies for HFrEF.Potassium binders: Recommended for patients with HF with hyperkalemia while taking RAASi.

ICDs and CRTs.Recommendation 1: In patients with nonischemic DCM or ischemic heart disease at least 40 days post-MI with LVEF ≤35% and NYHA class II or III symptoms on chronic GDMT, ICD therapy is recommended for primary prevention of SCD to reduce total mortality.
ICDs and CRTs.Value Statement: A transvenous ICD is economically valuable for primary prevention of SCD when the risk of death is high and the risk of nonarrhythmic death is deemed low.
ICDs and CRTs.Recommendation 3: In patients at least 40 days post-MI with LVEF ≤30% and NYHA class I symptoms, ICD therapy is recommended for primary prevention of SCD to reduce total mortality.

guidelines.recommendation: Patients with LVEF ≤35%, sinus rhythm, LBBB with QRS duration ≥150 ms, and NYHA class II, III, or ambulatory IV symptoms on GDMT should receive CRT.
guidelines.benefit: Reduce total mortality, reduce hospitalizations, and improve symptoms and QOL.

guidelines.recommendation: Patients with LVEF ≤35%, sinus rhythm, LBBB with a QRS duration of 120 to 149 ms, and NYHA class II, III, or ambulatory IV symptoms on GDMT can be considered for CRT.
guidelines.benefit: Reduce total mortality, reduce hospitalizations, and improve symptoms and QOL.

guidelines.recommendation: Patients with high-degree or complete heart block and LVEF of 36% to 50% could use CRT.
guidelines.benefit: Reduce total mortality, reduce hospitalizations, and improve symptoms and QOL.

guidelines.recommendation: Patients with LVEF ≤30%, ischemic cause of HF, sinus rhythm, LBBB with QRS ≥150 ms, and NYHA class I symptoms on GDMT may benefit from CRT.
guidelines.benefit: Reduce hospitalizations and improve symptoms and QOL.

guidelines.recommendation: Patients with genetic arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy with LVEF ≤45% could get ICD implantation.
CRT not recommended for patients with QRS duration <120 ms.
Patients with NYHA class I or II symptoms and non-LBBB with QRS <150 ms not recommended for CRT.

content.additional_notes: RCTs have guided ICD and CRT device decisions for 20 years, with subgroup analyses supplementing findings.

genes.context: LVEF ≤35%, LVEF of 36% to 50%, LVEF ≤30%, LVEF ≤45%

content.ICD Benefit: ICDs are beneficial in patients with previous MI, LVEF ≤35%, particularly in populations at risk of SCD. Several trials (MADIT, MUSTT, DEFINITE, SCD-HeFT, DANISH) are mentioned, showing benefit or specific findings regarding ICD implementation.
content.CRT Benefit: CRT improves outcomes in heart failure patients, with reductions in death or hospitalization noted in trials like MIRACLE, COMPANION, CARE-HF, and REVERSE. CRT is effective particularly in patients with QRS >120 ms and suitable functional classifications.
content.Cost-Effectiveness: ICD and CRT are generally cost-effective, improving life expectancy and quality of life. The economic evaluation may vary depending on the patient population.
content.Trials: The guideline discusses multiple pivotal trials such as MADIT, MADIT-II, MUSTT, DEFINITE, SCD-HeFT, DANISH, MIRACLE, COMPANION, CARE-HF, and REVERSE, highlighting the benefits of ICDs and CRT.

content.cost-effectiveness: The incremental cost-effectiveness for CRT remains <$150,000 per QALY with intermediate value for patients with NYHA class I to II.
content.LBBB benefits: Most benefit from CRT with LBBB and QRS durations >150 ms.
content.LVEF consideration: Extension of benefit for LVEF between 35% and 50% noted.
content.patients with AF: Included in several trials like MUSTIC, RAFT; benefits in COMPANION and MADIT-CRT.
content.ICD trials: 1-year survival as standard inclusion for ICD and CRT trials.

genes.context: Patients with LMNA/C cardiomyopathy will progress to cardiac transplantation, sometimes precipitated by refractory arrhythmias more than by pump failure.
Variants such as LMNA/C, desmosomal proteins, phospholamban, and Filamin-C carry implications for sudden death prevention.
Variants of Filamin-C have implications for ICD for sudden death prevention in patients.

CRT Recommendations: Patients with HF on GDMT for more than 3 months and more than 40 days if after MI, or with a special indication for pacing.
Evaluate general health status and comorbidities.
If LVEF ≤35% and NYHA class II–Amb class IV with LBBB ≥150 ms or non-LBBB 120–149 ms, consider CRT.
If LVEF 30% and ischemic CM with LBBB ≥150 ms, consider CRT.
If LVEF 36%–50%, consider CRT if there's a high degree or complete heart block.
For special circumstances with AF or NSR requiring frequent RV pacing, CRT may be considered.
Additional Notes: Autonomic nervous system modulation may be explored for its effects.

content.Title: Revascularization for CAD
content.Summary: The guideline recommends surgical revascularization plus GDMT for selected patients with heart failure (HF), reduced ejection fraction (EF ≤35%), and suitable coronary anatomy to improve symptoms, decrease cardiovascular hospitalizations, and reduce long-term all-cause mortality.
Key Points: Coronary artery disease (CAD) is associated with heart failure, often necessitating revascularization.
CABG surgery plus GDMT has shown long-term significance in reducing mortality and hospitalization despite not meeting primary endpoints at 56 months.
The STICH trial showed significant reductions in mortality with CABG+GDMT in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
Research supports CABG as beneficial over percutaneous coronary intervention in certain patients.
Surgical ventricular remodeling during CABG has not shown additional benefits.
GDMT and revascularization strategies may vary based on patient conditions, including HF hospitalization risk and ventricular function.

content.Synopsis: GDMT applies to all patients with HFrEF, irrespective of the presence of VHD. Management of significant valve disease should involve a multidisciplinary team with expertise in VHD. Optimization of GDMT can improve secondary MR associated with LV dysfunction. Patients with persistent severe secondary MR might require surgical or transcatheter repair after consultation with a cardiovascular team. TEER has been performed on patients with HFrEF and severe secondary MR.
content.Mitral Regurgitation: Optimization of GDMT and reassessment of MR before MV interventions are important. In certain cases, patients may benefit from either surgical or transcatheter repair. The COAPT trial showed significant reduction in HF in patients treated with TEER and GDMT compared to GDMT alone.
content.Aortic Stenosis: In patients with symptomatic aortic stenosis, transcatheter and surgical aortic valve repair can improve outcomes. TAVR UNLOAD evaluates the benefit of GDMT in nonsevere aortic stenosis and HFrEF.
content.Tricuspid Regurgitation: Management of secondary tricuspid regurgitation involves addressing underlying causes and may require referral to a multidisciplinary team.
content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: VHD is a significant contributor to HF. Management should be performed by a multidisciplinary team with expertise in HF and VHD to prevent worsening of HF and other adverse outcomes.

content.guideline: The guideline discusses the management of Secondary Mitral Regurgitation (SMR) in patients with heart failure. It emphasizes the use of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) including RAAS inhibitors, beta blockers, and biventricular pacing for improving mitral regurgitation (MR) and left ventricular (LV) dimensions. The COAPT trial findings are highlighted, showing benefits of Transcatheter Edge-to-Edge Repair (TEER) in patients with severe secondary MR, especially with left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) between 20% and 50%. A treatment approach is outlined which includes heart failure specialist-supervised GDMT and potential surgical interventions based on mitral anatomy and symptom severity.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with HFmrEF, SGLT2i can be beneficial in decreasing HF hospitalizations and cardiovascular mortality.

Among patients with current or previous symptomatic HFmrEF (LVEF, 41%-49%), use of evidence-based beta blockers, ARNi, ACEi, or ARB, and MRAs may be considered to reduce the risk of HF hospitalization and cardiovascular mortality, particularly among patients with LVEF on the lower end of this spectrum.

content.synopsis: There are no prospective RCTs for patients specifically with HFmrEF (LVEF, 41%-49%). All data are from post hoc or subsets of previous HF trials with patients now classified as HFmrEF. LVEF is a spectrum, and among patients with LVEF 41% to 49%, patients with LVEF on the lower end may respond to therapies similarly to HFpEF patients. Repeat evaluation of LVEF is advised to track disease progression. Future studies are needed for treatment clarification.
content.recommendation_specific_support: The EMPEROR-Preserved trial showed SGLT2i, empagliflozin, in patients with symptomatic HF, LVEF >40% and elevated natriuretic peptides, reduced HF hospitalization/cardiovascular death by 21%. In subgroup with LVEF 41% to 49%, empagliflozin reduced the risk of primary composite endpoint of cardiovascular death or hospitalization.

content.Clinical context: The document discusses guidelines for heart failure with improved ejection fraction (HFimpEF) and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF). It highlights the use of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) and the impact of various treatments such as beta-blockers, ARNIs, ACEi or ARBs, and spironolactone. The guidelines suggest regular evaluation of left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) and emphasize the continuation of GDMT to prevent relapse even in asymptomatic patients.

content.Synopsis: HFpEF (LVEF ≥50%) is prevalent, contributing to 50% of all HF cases. It's associated with significant morbidity and mortality. HFpEF is heterogeneous, involving comorbidities such as hypertension, diabetes, obesity, CAD, and CKD. It includes specific cases like cardiac amyloidosis. Treatment strategies involve medication and management of specific causes and comorbidities.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Patients with HFpEF and hypertension should have medication titrated to attain blood pressure targets in accord with clinical guidelines to prevent morbidity.
In patients with HFpEF, SGLT2i can be beneficial in decreasing HF hospitalizations and cardiovascular mortality.
In patients with HFpEF, management of AF can be useful to improve symptoms.
In selected patients with HFpEF, MRAs may decrease hospitalizations, particularly for those with LVEF at the lower end.
In selected patients with HFpEF, ARB use may decrease hospitalizations, especially for those with LVEF at the lower end.
In selected patients with HFpEF, ARNI may decrease hospitalizations, especially for those with LVEF at the lower end.
Routine use of nitrates or phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors in HFpEF to increase activity or QOL is ineffective.

content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Blood pressure control is crucial for HF prevention. The SPRINT trial showed intensive blood pressure control reduces HF and cardiovascular outcomes. Clinical guidelines provide specific targets for HFpEF patients.

content.primary_therapy: Beta blockers may be used to treat hypertension in patients with a history of MI, symptomatic CAD, or AF with rapid ventricular response.
content.emperor_trial: In symptomatic patients with HFpEF with LVEF >40% and elevated natriuretic peptides, SGLT2i empagliflozin showed a significant benefit, reducing HF hospitalizations and cardiovascular death by 21%.
content.current_trial_guidance: No large, randomized clinical trials are available for therapy in patients with HFpEF and AF. Guidelines for AF treatment in these patients can be extrapolated from general AF management.
content.mrant_usage: SPIR-HFPEF Reduced effects on mortality in a trial setting; careful monitoring of potassium, renal function, and diuretic dosing is advised to minimize risks.
content.raas_inhibition_observations: RAAS agents like ACEi, ARB, MRA, and ARNis might be first-line agents in HFpEF therapy, although clinical trials have not shown much benefit in patients with HFpEF.

content.text: The guideline discusses various treatments and trials for Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF). It includes information about different drugs and therapies, such as candesartan, sacubitril-valsartan, ARNIs (Angiotensin Receptor-Neprilysin Inhibitors), and nitrates, and their effects on outcomes like cardiovascular mortality, hospitalization, and NT-proBNP levels. Specific trials mentioned include PARAMOUNT-HF, PARAGON-HF, and NEAT-HFpEF. The guideline highlights that some treatments showed benefits in specific subgroups, like those with lower Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF), while others did not achieve significant improvements. It also recommends certain medications for patients with symptomatic HF and LVEF ≥50%.

content.context: ATTR can be caused by pathogenic variants in the transthyretin gene TTR. Genetic testing with TTR gene sequencing is recommended to differentiate hereditary variant from wild-type transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis. If ATTR-CM is identified, then genetic sequencing of the TTR gene will determine if the patient has a pathological variant (ATTRv) or wild-type (ATTRwt) disease.

recommendations: In select patients with wild-type or variant transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis and NYHA class I to III HF symptoms, tafamidis is indicated to reduce cardiovascular morbidity and mortality.
At 2020 list prices, tafamidis provides low economic value in patients with HF with wild-type or variant transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis.
In patients with cardiac amyloidosis and AF, anticoagulation is reasonable to reduce the risk of stroke regardless of the CHA2DS2-VASc scores.
content.synopsis: For patients with ATTR-CM and EF ≤40%, GDMT may be poorly tolerated. The vasodilating effects of ARNis, ACEis, and ARBs may exacerbate hypotension. Disease-modifying therapies include TTR silencers, TTR stabilizers, and TTR disruptors. Light chain cardiac amyloidosis is managed by specialists.
content.tafamidis: Tafamidis binds the thyroxin-binding site of TTR. It has shown benefits in clinical trials but provides low economic value due to its high cost. Tafamidis is available in different dosages.

genes.context: Tafamidis binds the thyroxin-binding site of TTR. Disease-modifying therapies include TTR silencers, TTR stabilizers, and TTR disruptors.

content.Title: Diagnostic and Treatment of Transthyretin Cardiac Amyloidosis Algorithm
Steps: History, ECG, echocardiogram, cardiac MRI suggestive of cardiac amyloidosis
If presence of a monoclonal light chain: Conduct hematology-oncology consultation and consider heart or other biopsy
If no monoclonal light chain: Check Tc-99m-PYP scan
If Tc-99m-PYP abnormal: Perform TTR gene sequencing
If amyloid on heart biopsy triggers different paths: AL-CM (amyloid cardiomyopathy) vs ATTR-CM (transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy)
Treatment recommendations based on subtype of amyloidosis (ATTRv-CM, ATTRwt-CM)
Additional treatments include antidotes like Tafamidis
Anticoagulation regardless of CHA2DS2-VASc score in case of atrial fibrillation

genes.context: Perform TTR gene sequencing

content.clinical_guideline: The guideline discusses heart failure (HF) and the importance of tailored referrals for advanced HF to optimize patient outcomes. Key considerations include symptom management and timely referrals to specialists for advanced therapies such as LVAD, cardiac transplantation, and palliative care. The document emphasizes that HF patients should be evaluated for nonadherence to medications. Refractory symptoms and a comprehensive multidisciplinary assessment guide decisions around advanced therapies.

recommendations.Recommendation: Patients with advanced HF should be referred to specialized care for HF management and to assess suitability for advanced HF therapies.

advanced_HF_criteria: New York Heart Association class IIIB to IV or elevated natriuretic peptides
Low systolic BP and high heart rate

Table 16: ESC Definition of Advanced HF.intro: All these criteria must be present despite optimal guideline-directed treatment.

criteria.1: Severe and persistent symptoms of HF (NYHA class III [advanced] or IV).

2.description: Severe cardiac dysfunction defined by ≥1 of these:
items: EF ≥40%, elevated natriuretic peptide levels and evidence of significant diastolic dysfunction

3.description: Hospitalizations or unplanned visits in the past 12 mo for episodes of:
items: Congestion requiring high-dose intravenous diuretics or diuretic combinations
Low output requiring inotropes or vasoactive medications

criteria.4: Severe impairment of exercise capacity with inability to exercise or low 6-minute walk test distance (<300 m) or peak VO2 (<12–14 mL/kg/min) estimated to be of cardiac origin.

Table 16: ESC Definition of Advanced HF.notes: Criteria 1 and 4 can be met in patients with cardiac dysfunction (as described in criterion 2) but who also have substantial limitations as a result of other conditions (eg, severe pulmonary disease, noncardiac cirrhosis, renal disease). The therapeutic options for these patients may be more limited.
Table 16: ESC Definition of Advanced HF.source_note: Adapted with permission from Crespo-Leiro et al.

Table 17: INTERMACS Profiles.overview: Profiles describe severity/stability of patients with advanced heart failure and features of each profile.

profiles.Features: Life-threatening hypotension and rapidly escalating inotropic/pressor support, with critical organ hypoperfusion often confirmed by worsening acidosis and lactate levels.

"Dependent" on inotropic support but shows signs of continuing deterioration in nutrition, renal function, fluid retention, or other major status indicators. Can apply to a patient with refractory volume overload, evidence of impaired perfusion, in whom inotropic infusions cannot be maintained because of tachyarrhythmias, clinical ischemia, or other intolerance.

Clinically stable on mild-moderate doses of intravenous inotropes (or has a temporary circulatory support device) after repeated documentation of failure to wean without symptomatic hypotension, worsening symptoms, or progressive organ dysfunction (usually renal).

profiles.Profile Description: Resting symptoms on oral therapy at home
profiles.Features: Patient at home on oral therapy but frequently has symptoms of congestion at rest or with activities of daily living (dressing or bathing). May have orthopnea, shortness of breath during dressing/bathing, gastrointestinal symptoms, disabling ascites, or severe lower extremity edema.
Patient comfortable at rest but unable to engage in any activity, living predominantly within the house or housebound.

Patient comfortable at rest without evidence of fluid overload but able to do some mild activity. Activities of daily living are comfortable; minor activities outside the home such as visiting friends or going to a restaurant can be performed, but fatigue results within a few minutes or with any meaningful physical exertion.

Clinically stable with a reasonable level of comfortable activity despite history of previous decompensation not recent. Usually able to walk more than a block. Any recent decompensation requiring IV diuretics or hospitalization in previous month should categorize the patient as Profile 6 or lower.

Table 17: INTERMACS Profiles.notes: Adapted from Stevenson et al.; profiles 3 to 6 may be modified for patients with recurrent decompensations leading to frequent hospital/ED visits or for other modifiers such as arrhythmia. ICD indicates implantable cardioverter-defibrillator; INTERMACS, Interagency Registry for Mechanically Assisted Circulatory Support; NYHA, New York Heart Association.

Clinical Indicators of Advanced HF: Repeated hospitalizations or emergency department visits for HF in the past 12 months.
Persistent NYHA functional class III to IV symptoms despite therapy.
Severely reduced exercise capacity (peak VO2 <14 mL/kg/min or <50% predicted, 6-min walk test distance <300 m, or inability to walk 1 block on level ground).
Intolerance to RAASi because of hypotension or worsening renal function.
Intolerance to beta blockers due to worsening HF or hypotension.
Escalation of diuretics, needing daily furosemide or metolazone therapy.
Progressive deterioration in renal or hepatic function.
Worsening right HF or secondary pulmonary hypertension.
Refractory or recurrent ventricular arrhythmias, frequent ICD shocks.
Increased predicted 1-year mortality (>20%) in HF survival models (MAGGIC, SHFM).

Indications: NYHA class IIIb to IV functional limitations despite maximal therapy.
End-organ dysfunction due to low cardiac output.
Relative Contraindications: Active systemic infection or prolonged intubation.

content.Nonpharmacological Management Summary: For patients with advanced HF and hyponatremia, the benefit of fluid restriction to reduce congestive symptoms is uncertain.

Part 8.2 Details.Fluid Restriction: Common for patients with hyponatremia in acute HF; modest improvement observed.
Part 8.2 Details.Sodium Restriction: Evidence is low in quality; limited effect on clinical outcomes or diuretic use in advanced HF.
Part 8.2 Details.Nutritional Counseling: Focus on restricting sodium and fluid to prevent cachexia or malnutrition.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Fluid restriction in acute decompensated HF improved hyponatremia marginally.
RCTs on fluid restriction showed no reduction in hospitalization or mortality rates.

Inotropic Support Summary.Positive Inotropic Agents: Have not shown improved survival in HF; use for symptom control as palliative care or bridge to transplantation.

Part 8.3 Details.Advanced HF Therapy: Continuous intravenous inotropic support recommended as bridge therapy for eligible patients.

content.title: Intravenous Inotropic Agents Used in the Management of HF
key_points: Intravenous inotropic support is used to maintain systemic perfusion and organ performance in patients with heart failure (HF).
The table compares commonly used inotropes based on their effects, adverse effects, and special considerations.
Adrenergic agonists include dopamine and dobutamine, while milrinone is a PDE3 inhibitor, and epinephrine and norepinephrine are vasopressors.
Adverse effects include hypertension, headaches, nausea, tissue necrosis, and others.
Selection of inotropic agent should consider potential adverse effects and the patient’s specific condition.
There is a lack of strong evidence favoring one agent over another, so usage is guided by the balance of benefits and risks.
Inotropes are considered for short-term support or bridge therapy in heart failure management.

content.Summary: MCS is used for patients with advanced HFfEF to enhance lifespan and functional capacity. Both temporary and durable options exist, differing in terms of location, approach, flow characteristics, pump mechanisms, and supported ventricles. Durable LVADs show improved survival and functional outcomes, especially with NYHA class IV patients.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with advanced HFfEF with NYHA class IV symptoms on intravenous inotropes or temporary MCS, durable LVAD implantation is effective for improving functional status, QoL, and survival.
In patients with advanced HFfEF and NYHA class IV symptoms despite GDMT, durable MCS benefits include improved symptoms and reduced mortality.
In such patients, durable MCS provides low to intermediate economic value based on costs and outcomes.
In patients with hemodynamic compromise and shock, temporary MCS is reasonable as a 'bridge to recovery' or 'bridge to decision'.

content.topic: Cardiac Transplantation for Advanced Heart Failure

recommendations.recommendation: For selected patients with advanced HF despite GDMT, cardiac transplantation is indicated to improve survival and QOL.
In patients with stage D advanced HF despite GDMT, cardiac transplantation provides intermediate economic value.

content.synopsis: Cardiac transplantation is effective for improving mortality and morbidity for selected stage D HF patients. Median survival of adult transplant recipients now exceeds 12 years. Developments in donor allocation policy and management have reduced waitlist mortality and improved posttransplant outcomes.
content.additional_notes: Temporary MCS helps stabilize patients before deciding on further treatment. The cost-effectiveness of ventricular assist devices was evaluated and found to be of low economic value.

Sections.Title: Assessment of Patients Hospitalized With Decompensated HF
Sections.Content: Patients hospitalized with heart failure (HF) should have assessments for severity of congestion and adequacy of perfusion to guide therapy. This includes identifying any comorbidities or precipitating factors for heart failure.

Sections.Title: Synopsis
Sections.Content: Initial triage of HF includes clinical assessment of hemodynamic profile. Cardiogenic shock requires further evaluation. In ischemic disease, urgent revascularization might be needed. Some conditions, like pulmonary edema and severe hypertension, may require immediate treatment to reduce pressure.

Sections.Title: Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text
Sections.Content: Patients with HF have clinical evidence of congestion without inadequate perfusion. Some may present with a mismatch in right- and left-sided cardiac filling pressures. This requires tailored treatment approaches.

Transplantation.Improvements: Cardiac transplant improves functional status and health-related quality of life.

content.Table 21: Lists common factors precipitating hospitalization with acute decompensated heart failure (HF). Factors include ACS, uncontrolled hypertension, atrial fibrillation and other arrhythmias, additional cardiac disease, acute infections, nonadherence with medication regimen or dietary intake, anemia, hyper- or hypothyroidism, medications that increase sodium retention, and medications with negative inotropic effect.
content.Clinical Assessment: Jugular venous distention, edema, and elevated natriuretic peptides can help identify HF. However, they have less utility in certain situations, like obesity and HFpEF, or in the setting of sepsis.
content.Hypoperfusion: Often underappreciated in chronic HF patients. Indicated by narrow pulse pressure, cool extremities, and elevated serum lactate levels.
content.Hospitalization for HF: Signals worsening prognosis and provides opportunities to redirect disease trajectory. Management should include addressing precipitating factors and previous limitations in disease management.
GDMT Recommendations During Hospitalizat: Preexisting GDMT should be continued and optimized.
In cases of mild renal function decline or asymptomatic blood pressure reduction, diuresis and other GDMT should not be routinely discontinued.
GDMT should be initiated during hospitalization after clinical stability is achieved.
If GDMT discontinuation is necessary, it should be reinitiated and optimized as soon as possible.

content.Summary: Hospitalization for HFrEF (Heart Failure with reduced Ejection Fraction) is an opportunity to optimize guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT). Several studies have shown the benefits of continuing GDMT during hospitalization for heart failure (HF). However, adherence to GDMT post-discharge remains suboptimal. Recommendations from various trials and registries are discussed, emphasizing the continuation of GDMT.

Recommendations.Findings: Discontinuation of beta blockers was associated with higher risk in-hospital mortality. Caution is advised when discontinuing these therapies, especially in patients with marked volume overload.
Withdrawal of ACEi-ARB was associated with higher risk of post-discharge mortality and readmission.
Discontinuation of oral GDMT was associated with higher mortality risk.
Few patients achieved target doses of beta blockers or ACEi-ARB.

content.Additional Notes: Stopping medications such as spironolactone and beta blockers in certain conditions can worsen renal function and heart failure morbidity. Using nebivolol in elderly patients showed equivalent outcomes regardless of systolic blood pressure (SBP) levels.

recommendations.Recommendation: Patients with HF admitted with evidence of significant fluid overload should be promptly treated with intravenous loop diuretics to improve symptoms and reduce morbidity.

For patients hospitalized with HF, therapy with diuretics and other guideline-directed medications should be titrated with a goal to resolve clinical evidence of congestion to reduce symptoms and rehospitalizations.
For patients requiring diuretic treatment during hospitalization for HF, the discharge regimen should include a plan for adjustment of diuretics to decrease rehospitalizations.

In patients hospitalized with HF when diuresis is inadequate to relieve symptoms and signs of congestion, it is reasonable to intensify the diuretic regimen using either a higher doses of intravenous loop diuretics or b addition of a second diuretic.

content.Synopsis: Intravenous loop diuretic therapy provides rapid and effective treatment for signs and symptoms of congestion leading to hospitalization for HF. Effective diuresis may require doubling of initial doses or adding a thiazide diuretic or an MRA. This therapy aims to resolve symptoms of congestion to prevent rehospitalizations and mortality. Loop diuretics should be prescribed at discharge to prevent symptom recurrence.

content.General Information: The guideline discusses the management of heart failure (HF) with a focus on diuretic and vasodilator therapies. Patients with recent HF hospitalization often need diuretics after discharge to prevent fluid retention. Use of ACEi, ARBs, MRAs, and beta-blockers may reduce recurrent congestion leading to hospitalization. The titration of diuretics, including serial doubling of doses and infusion methods, is recommended to achieve effects. Parenteral vasodilation, using nitroglycerin or nitroprusside, can be considered for HF patients without symptomatic hypotension as an adjunct to diuretic therapy.

Recommendation 1.Description: In patients hospitalized with HF, prophylaxis for VTE is recommended to prevent venous thromboembolic disease.

Recommendation VTE Prophylaxis in Hospit.Synopsis: HF is recognized as an additional risk for venous thromboembolic disease. When patients are hospitalized for decompensated HF or for other reasons, the risk is elevated. Anticoagulation use is advised, including low-molecular-weight heparin, fondaparinux, or approved DOAC.

Recommendation 1.Description: For patients with cardiogenic shock, intravenous inotropic support should be used to maintain systemic perfusion and preserve end-organ performance.

Recommendation 2.Description: For patients with cardiogenic shock, temporary MCS is reasonable when end-organ function cannot be maintained by pharmacologic means to support cardiac function.

content.Synopsis: Cardiogenic shock is a critical condition characterized by reduced cardiac output and end-organ dysfunction, commonly identified by hypotension (SBP <90 mm Hg). It typically accompanies advanced heart failure or myocardial dysfunction without significant history of heart failure, acute myocardial infarction (MI), or post-cardiac surgery. Management involves early recognition, invasive hemodynamic assessment, and utilization of pharmacological and mechanical circulatory support (MCS) systems.
Recommendations: Consideration of pulmonary artery (PA) line placement in some cases to guide hemodynamic management.
For unreceptive patients to initial measures, triaging to centers with MCS capability may be necessary.
Intravenous inotropic support can be used to enhance cardiac output in appropriate cases.

Clinical Criteria.Shock Clinical Criteria: SBP <90 mm Hg for >30 min, with possible need for vasopressors or additional criteria like reduced urine output.
Clinical Criteria.Hemodynamic Criteria: SBP <90 mm Hg or mean BP <60 mm Hg, cardiac index <2.2 L/min/m², pulmonary capillary wedge pressure >15 mm Hg, and other considerations like cardiac power output and shock index.

content.Note: The guideline highlights limitations in evidence for specific therapeutic interventions and suggests a tailored approach based on clinical presentation and available resources.

Stages.Bedside Findings: Normotensive, Normal venous pressure, Clear lungs, Warm extremities, Strong palpable pulses, Normal mentation
Hypotension, Elevated venous pressure, Rales present, Warm extremities, Strong pulses, Normal mentation
Stages.Selected Laboratory Markers: Preserved renal function, Normal lactate, Elevated BNP
Stages.Hemodynamics: SBP <90 mm Hg, MAP <60 mm Hg, or >30 mm Hg decrease from baseline SBP, HR >100 bpm, Hemodynamics: CI ≥2.2 L/min/m²

Stages.Bedside Findings: Hypotension, Hypoperfusion, Elevated venous pressure, Rales present, Cold, ashen, diaphoretic, Weak or nonpalpable pulses, Altered mentation, Decreased urine output, Respiratory distress
Stages.Selected Laboratory Markers: Impaired renal function, Increased lactate, Elevated BNP, Increased LFTs, Acidosis
Stages.Hemodynamics: SBP <90 mm Hg; MAP <60 mm Hg; >30 mm Hg from baseline SBP despite drugs and temporary MCS, HR >100 bpm, Hemodynamics: CI ≤2.2 L/min/m²; PCWP >15 mm Hg; CPO <0.6 W; PAPI <2.0; CVP:PCWI >1.0

Stages.Bedside Findings: Worsening hypotension, Worsening hypoperfusion, Same as stage C
Stages.Selected Laboratory Markers: Persistent or worsening values of stage C
Stages.Hemodynamics: Escalating use of pressors or MCS to maintain SBP and end-organ perfusion in setting of stage C hemodynamics

Stages.Bedside Findings: Refractory hypotension, Refractory hypoperfusion, Cardiac arrest, CPR
Stages.Selected Laboratory Markers: Worsening values of stage C laboratories
Stages.Hemodynamics: SBP only with resuscitation, PEA, Recurrent VT/VF

content.title: Heart Failure Guidelines
content.summary: The guideline discusses recommendations for the integration of care, transitions, and team-based approaches for heart failure (HF) management. It emphasizes the importance of multidisciplinary HF disease management programs, patient-centered discharge instructions, participation in benchmarking systems, and early follow-up post-hospitalization.

recommendations.Details: In patients with high-risk HF, referral to multidisciplinary HF disease management programs is recommended to reduce hospitalizations.
In hospitalized patients with worsening HF, patient-centered discharge instructions with a clear transitional care plan are essential before discharge.
Participation in systems that allow for benchmarking to performance measures is reasonable to enhance evidence-based therapy and care quality.
Early follow-up, usually within 7 days of discharge, is reasonable to optimize care and reduce rehospitalization.

content.synopsis: Transition from inpatient to outpatient care in HF patients is crucial due to the progressive disease state and complex medical regimens. Optimal transitions can reduce readmissions and improve satisfaction.

content.guideline: This document outlines important components of a transitional care plan for patients with heart failure (HF). Key areas include addressing causes of worsening HF, adjusting diuretics, coordinating laboratory checks, updating GDMT (guideline-directed medical therapy), reinforcing HF education, addressing high-risk characteristics, and referrals for additional care. The guideline emphasizes the importance of comprehensive discharge planning and postdischarge follow-up to improve outcomes and reduce rehospitalization.

content.Title: Management of Comorbidities in Patients With Heart Failure (HF)

Recommendations.Category: Management of Anemia or Iron Deficiency

Recommendations.Text: In patients with HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and iron deficiency with or without anemia, intravenous iron replacement is reasonable to improve functional status and quality of life (QOL).
In patients with HF and anemia, erythropoietin-stimulating agents should not be used to improve morbidity and mortality.
In patients with HFrEF and hypertension, uptitration of GDMT to the maximally tolerated target dose is recommended.
In patients with HF and suspicion of sleep-disordered breathing, a formal sleep assessment is reasonable to confirm the diagnosis and differentiate between obstructive and central sleep apnea.
In patients with HF and obstructive sleep apnea, continuous positive airway pressure may be reasonable to improve sleep quality and decrease daytime sleepiness.
In patients with NYHA class II to IV HFrEF and central sleep apnea, adaptive servo-ventilation causes harm.
In patients with HF and type 2 diabetes, the use of SGLT2i is recommended for the management of hyperglycemia and to reduce HF-related morbidity and mortality.

content.Synopsis: Multimorbidity is common in patients with HF, with >85% of patients having ≥2 additional chronic conditions like hypertension, ischemic heart disease, diabetes, anemia, CKD, morbid obesity, frailty, and malnutrition, which complicate HF management and prognosis.

Details: Routine baseline assessment of all patients with HF includes an evaluation for anemia, which is independently associated with HF disease severity and decreases response to treatment.

content.title: Recommendations for Treatment of Patients With HF and Selected Comorbidities

additional_therapies.recommendation: Optimal treatment according to hypertension guidelines (1)

additional_therapies.patients: Patients with HF and type 2 diabetes
Select patients with HF and LVEF ≤35% and suitable coronary anatomy
Patients with HF attributable to VHD or cancer therapy
Select patients with HF and AF

specific_treatments.condition: Patients with HFrEF and iron deficiency

specific_treatments.treatment: AV nodal ablation and CRT implantation (2a)

specific_treatments.condition: Patients with HF and symptoms attributable to AF
Patients with HF with obstructive sleep apnea
Asymptomatic patients with cancer therapy-related cardiomyopathy (EF <50%)

content.title: Most Common Co-Occurring Chronic Conditions Among Medicare Beneficiaries With Heart Failure
content.anemia_treatment: Erythropoietin-stimulating agent therapy is not recommended for the treatment of anemia in patients with HF.
content.hypertension: Antihypertensive therapy is associated with a decrease in the risk of incident HF in the general population. Hypertension in patients with HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) represents an opportunity to maximize GDMT to goal blood pressures defined by the ACC/AHA hypertension guidelines.

sleep_disorders.daytime_sleepiness: Typically a feature of obstructive sleep apnea and may not reflect the degree of underlying sleep-disordered breathing.
sleep_disorders.positive_airway_pressure: Associated with better sleep quality and nocturnal oxygenation but not shown to affect survival.
sleep_disorders.adaptive_servo-ventilation: Associated with increased mortality in HFrEF patients with central sleep apnea.

Diabetes.Guidelines: The American Diabetes Association guidelines recommend the use of SGLT2i as a first-line agent for the treatment of hyperglycemia in patients with diabetes with HF or at high risk of HF.
Diabetes.SGLT2i: SGLT2 inhibitors are associated with a reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events, including hospitalization for HF and cardiovascular death.
Diabetes.Mechanism: The mechanisms underlying the improvement in cardiovascular outcomes attributed to SGLT2i are partially related to the glucosuric effect.
Diabetes.Trials: Two RCTs totaling 8474 patients with NYHA class II, III, or IV HF and EF ≤40%—DAPA-HF assessing dapagliflozin and EMPEROR-Reduced assessing empagliflozin—showed significant reductions in relative risk of all-cause death (13%), cardiovascular death (14%), hospitalization for HF (26%), and renal outcomes (38%) with SGLT2i treatment.

Recommendations: Patients with chronic HF with permanent-persistent-paroxysmal AF and a CHA2DS2-VASc score of ≥2 (for men) and ≥3 (for women) should receive chronic anticoagulant therapy.
DOAC is recommended over warfarin in eligible patients.
AF ablation is reasonable to improve symptoms and QOL.
If rhythm control strategy fails or is not desired, atrioventricular node ablation with implantation of a CRT device is reasonable.
Chronic anticoagulant therapy is reasonable for men and women without additional risk factors.

Recommendation.1: Long-term warfarin for stroke prevention in AF patients is well established according to the AHA/ACC/Heart Rhythm Society guidelines for patients with certain risk factors.
Recommendation.2: DOACs have proven efficacy and safety compared to warfarin, recommended for patients with elevated CHA₂DS₂-VASc scores.
Recommendation.3: Ablation in HF patients has shown improved hospitalization and mortality outcomes.
Recommendation.4: Atrioventricular nodal ablation with CRT is considered a treatment option when other controls fail.
Recommendation.5: Heart failure is a major risk factor for stroke and systemic embolism, necessitating anticoagulation.

content.title: 11. SPECIAL POPULATIONS

recommendations.recommendation: In vulnerable patient populations at risk for health disparities, HF risk assessments and multidisciplinary management strategies should target both known risks for CVD and social determinants of health, as a means toward elimination of disparate HF outcomes.
Evidence of health disparities should be monitored and addressed at the clinical practice and the health care system levels.

content.synopsis: There are important differences in HF incidence, risk factors, clinical care needs, and outcomes between specific patient populations. HF is highest in self-identified Black patients, and mortality rates for Black patients are also higher than White patients. Other patients at risk include older patients, women, and those with social and medical vulnerabilities such as low income and lack of caregiver support. Social determinants like socioeconomic status and accessibility to care are key factors influencing disparities in HF outcomes. Homelessness, substance use, and food insecurity are additional factors posing risks. Enhanced care strategies are vital for coordination across multiple health sectors.

Women.Risk of HF: Higher lifetime risk of HF in women than men. Odds of HFpEF are 2.8-fold higher in women.
Women.HF Outcomes: Favorable survival with HF than men, but women less likely to receive GDMT.

Older Adults.Risk of HF: For ages 40 and older, lifetime risk is 20%. LVEF is preserved in two-thirds with HF.
Older Adults.HF Outcomes: 40% mortality at 27 months follow-up at age 80 and above.

Low Socioeconomic Status Populations.Risk of HF: Neighborhood deprivation increases HF risk by 12%.
Low Socioeconomic Status Populations.HF Outcomes: Higher mortality with lower socioeconomic status. Gradient seen across quartiles.

Black Populations.Risk of HF: Highest risk of incident HF and proportion of nonischemic HF.
Black Populations.HF Outcomes: Higher age-adjusted HF-related CVD death rate.

Hispanic Populations.Risk of HF: Higher HF incidence than non-Hispanic Whites.

Asian and Pacific Islander Populations.HF Outcomes: Higher rates of preventable HF hospitalizations.

Native American and Alaskan Native Popul.Risk of HF: Limited data; risk factors include hypertension and diabetes.
Native American and Alaskan Native Popul.HF Outcomes: HF mortality similar to White populations.

Recommendations: In patients who develop cancer therapy-related cardiomyopathy or HF, a multidisciplinary discussion involving the patient about the risk-benefit ratio of cancer therapy interruption, discontinuation, or continuation is recommended.
In asymptomatic patients with cancer therapy-related cardiomyopathy (EF <50%), ARB, ACEi, and beta blockers are reasonable to prevent progression to HF and improve cardiac function.

In patients with cardiovascular risk factors or known cardiac disease being considered for potentially cardiotoxic anticancer therapies, pretherapy evaluation of cardiac function is recommended to establish baseline cardiac function and guide therapy choice.

Monitoring of cardiac function is reasonable in patients with cardiovascular risk factors or known cardiac disease receiving potentially cardiotoxic anticancer therapies for early identification of drug-induced cardiomyopathy.
Initiation of beta blockers and ACEi/ARB for the primary prevention of drug-induced cardiomyopathy in patients at risk of cancer therapy-related cardiomyopathy is of uncertain benefit.
Serial measurement of cardiac troponin might be recommended for stratification in patients being considered for potentially cardiotoxic therapies.
content.Synopsis: Cardiovascular complications from cancer therapy such as cardiomyopathy and HF can significantly impact survival. Advances in cancer therapy and an aging population increase the number of cancer patients with cardiovascular disease. Guidelines emphasize pretherapy evaluation, early detection, and treatment of cardiomyopathy in cancer patients.

content.General Information: The guidelines focus on cancer therapies associated with cardiomyopathy, detailing the importance of cardiac function monitoring, especially with therapies like anthracyclines and anti-HER2 agents. Pretherapy assessments and managing preexisting cardiomyopathy are emphasized. The document highlights various risk factors for cancer therapy-related cardiomyopathy, including age, preexisting conditions, and prior exposure to potentially cardiotoxic therapies.

genes.context: Studying the effectiveness of neurohormonal therapies specifically in patients with the CTRC gene is challenging given the relative infrequency of events, heterogeneity of offending agents, the poorly understood pathophysiology, and the overlap with comorbid CVD.

concepts: The pretherapy evaluation can help identify patients at risk for therapy-related cardiomyopathy.
Monitoring LVEF for patients receiving potentially cardiotoxic anticancer agents is to identify cardiac injury and manage it accordingly.
Beta blockers and ACEI-ARB are utilized to aid recovery of LVEF.
There is debate about the effectiveness of preemptive use of therapies like ACEI-ARB in reducing cardiomyopathy risk.
Biomarkers like troponin can be used for risk stratification in patients undergoing cardiotoxic therapies.

recommendations.recommendation: In women with a history of HF or cardiomyopathy, patient-centered counseling regarding contraception and the risks of cardiovascular deterioration during pregnancy is recommended.

genes.context: In a study of 2625 patients receiving anthracyclines for breast cancer or lymphoma who underwent serial LVEF monitoring, cancer therapy-related cardiomyopathy occurred in 9% of patients.

content.HF complication: HF may complicate pregnancy either secondary to an existing pregnancy cardiomyopathy or as a result of peripartum cardiomyopathy.
content.Peripartum cardiomyopathy definition: Defined as systolic dysfunction, typically LVEF <45%, often with LV dilation, occurring in late pregnancy or early postpartum.
content.Incidence: 1 in 1000 to 8000 deliveries in the United States; higher rates in Nigeria, Haiti, and South Africa.
content.Associated factors: Maternal age >30 years, African ancestry, multiparity, multigestation, preeclampsia/eclampsia, anemia, diabetes, obesity, and prolonged tocolysis.
content.Genetic contribution: Noted particularly with titan gene mutations.
content.Treatment recommendations: GDMT adjusted for pregnancy or breastfeeding, anticoagulation consideration, and detection of pathogenic 16-kDa prolactin.
content.Prognosis: Related to initial LVEF, LV thrombus, RV involvement, preeclampsia, geographic region, and race.
content.Pregnancy outcomes: Severe HF may require anticoagulation, especially in the first 6 to 8 weeks postpartum.

genes.context: A genetic contribution is recognized, particularly with titan gene mutations.

Nonpharmacological strategies.Preconception: Counseling and testing for heritable cardiac conditions. Use cardiovascular risk tools and echocardiography.
Nonpharmacological strategies.During Pregnancy: Monitor for HF signs or cardiovascular instability. Consider echocardiographic screening in the third trimester.
Nonpharmacological strategies.Postpartum: Recommendations from obstetrics and neonatology. Management should include hemodynamic monitoring.

Pharmacological strategies.Preconception: Review medications, modify HF pharmacotherapy to minimize risk of placental hypoperfusion. Repeat echocardiography if needed.
Pharmacological strategies.During Pregnancy: Monitor blood pressure, heart rate, volume status. Adjust doses appropriately.
Pharmacological strategies.Postpartum: For patients with PPCM and LVEF <35%, initiate GDMT including beta blockers and anticoagulation if appropriate.

Multidisciplinary care.Preconception: Consultation with genetics, gynecology, and relevant teams.
Multidisciplinary care.During Pregnancy: Management with obstetrics and maternal-fetal medicine teams.
Multidisciplinary care.Postpartum: Management with neonatology and obstetrics teams.

content.summary: The guideline discusses treatment and recommendations for acute peripartum cardiomyopathy, particularly regarding the safety and effectiveness of specific heart failure treatments during pregnancy and lactation. Key recommendations include the use of empirically tested performance measures to improve care and participation in quality improvement programs. Certain medications, such as ACEi, ARB, and spironolactone, are contraindicated or have usage restrictions due to potential risks during pregnancy. Postpartum women may safely use certain medications like ACEi and beta blockers. The importance of quality metrics and reporting, particularly in hospitals, is emphasized to improve heart failure outcomes.

recommendation.Details: For all patients with HF, palliative and supportive care—including high-quality communication, conveyance of prognosis, clarifying goals of care, shared decision-making, symptom management, and caregiver support—should be provided to improve QOL and relieve suffering (1).

content.Synopsis: Palliative care is patient- and family-centered care that optimizes health-related quality of life (QOL) by anticipating, preventing, and treating suffering. It includes communication, prognosis estimation, anticipatory guidance, decision-making, and support for emotional and psychological needs. It additionally involves the families in the care team and addresses bereavement.

Recommendations.Recommendation: For patients with heart failure (HF), the option of discontinuing life-extending therapies should be anticipated and discussed as medical conditions and goals of care change.
For advanced stage D HF patients, specialist palliative care consultation is advisable, especially for those needing inotropic support or mechanical support.
For patients with HF, executing advance care directives can help document treatment preferences and improve patient-centered care.
In patients with advanced HF and expected survival less than 6 months, timely referral to hospice can improve QOL.

Text: Application of palliative care principles can improve care processes and patient outcomes but does not necessarily require a formal palliative care consultation.
As illnesses progress, reevaluation of treatment goals, including deactivation of life-sustaining therapies, is important.
An interdisciplinary palliative approach should be a part of routine care for patients across all stages of HF.

Palliative and Supportive Care Domains.Communication: Central to palliative care approaches are communication and patient-caregiver engagement techniques.
Palliative and Supportive Care Domains.Conveyance of Prognosis: Addresses patient and caregiver understanding of disease, treatment, and prognosis, emphasizing the importance of realistic expectations.
Palliative and Supportive Care Domains.Clarifying Goals of Care: Includes discussions about treatment discontinuation, location of death, and hospice care for end-stage heart failure.
Palliative and Supportive Care Domains.Shared Decision-Making: Process by which patients and clinicians choose optimal health care decisions considering risks, benefits, and patient values.
Palliative and Supportive Care Domains.Symptom Management: Includes addressing dyspnea, fatigue, pain, nausea, and other symptoms of heart failure.
Palliative and Supportive Care Domains.Caregiver Support: Extends care to patient’s loved ones, offering support beyond death.

content.section_title: 14. Recommendation for Patient-Reported Outcomes and Evidence Gaps and Future Research Directions

subsections.title: 14.1. Patient-Reported Outcomes
subsections.recommendation: In patients with Heart Failure (HF), standardized assessment of patient-reported health status using a validated questionnaire can be useful to provide incremental information for patient functional status, symptom burden, and prognosis.

content.synopsis: Health status includes symptoms, functional status, and related outcomes. Traditionally, health status assessments have been based on clinical interviews and exams, using classifications like NYHA. Incorporating patient-reported health status offers additional insights beyond standard evaluation. There are various tools, such as standardized questionnaires, that are sensitive to changes in disease status.
content.recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Standardized patient-reported health status questionnaires are independent predictors of clinical outcomes and may have higher sensitivity and correlation with clinical outcomes compared to other functional status measures. They should be used to incorporate patient voice and improve health status understanding across practices.

Estimated PA systolic pressure > 35 mm Hg

notes: Cutoffs for natriuretic peptide levels may have lower specificity in older patients or in those with AF or CKD.
Higher cutoff values are recommended for these populations.
Natriuretic peptide cutoffs for population screening for pre-HF may be set < 99% reference limits.
